summary: This guide presents threat modeling as an essential security practice for software teams, advocating for a 'little and often' approach integrated into daily development rather than exhaustive upfront analysis. The core methodology involves tracing data flows through systems and systematically asking 'what can go wrong?' using the STRIDE framework (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege). The article provides two practical examples: a quick 15-30 minute whiteboard session for a single feature, and a larger cross-team workshop for platform infrastructure. The key outcome is translating identified threats into prioritized, actionable security stories in the team's backlog.
key_points: Start threat modeling by mapping data flows through your system, transforming vague security concerns into specific, actionable questions
Use STRIDE as a structured framework to systematically identify threats across six categories rather than relying on open-ended brainstorming
Integrate threat modeling in 'little and often' sessions tied to current work rather than attempting exhaustive big-bang analysis
Threat modeling is a team sport that benefits from diverse perspectives—developers, product owners, and fresh eyes often spot risks that security specialists miss
The goal isn't just finding threats but building shared security understanding and translating threats into concrete backlog items
money_quotes: The key to cutting through complexity in threat modeling lies in tracing how data moves through your technology stack.
Identifying threats can become deceptively simple: follow each one of the data flows and ask 'What can go wrong?'
It will force you to pivot from thinking 'how system works' to 'how system fails', which in essence is the crux of threat modeling.
Spending fifteen minutes examining the security implications of a new feature can yield more practical value than hours analyzing hypothetical scenarios for code that isn't written yet.
The goal isn't just to find threats - it's to build shared understanding.
Fresh eyes often spot risks that experts might miss, and every team member brings valuable context about how the system is built and used.
The goal of threat modeling isn't to instill paranoia, but to drive improvement.
No team has ever found this 'too hard' when approached step by step.
key_insight: Effective threat modeling transforms abstract security concerns into concrete, actionable improvements by systematically following data flows and asking 'what can go wrong?' in regular, bite-sized sessions rather than exhaustive workshops.
